title: BHP Signals Interest in Acquiring Mid-Tier Copper Developer as Organic Growth Falls Short
description: BHP Signals Interest in Acquiring Mid-Tier Copper Developer as Organic Growth Falls Short Australian Financial Review
htmlContent:
BHP Signals Interest in Acquiring Mid-Tier Copper Developer as Organic Growth Falls Short

By Peter Ker, Australian Financial Review • June 8, 2026

MELBOURNE – BHP chief executive Mike Henry has signalled the mining giant is open to acquiring a mid-tier copper developer to supplement its own organic growth pipeline, as investor pressure to grow production in line with surging prices and demand mounts.

Speaking at a metals and mining conference in Sydney on Monday, Mr Henry said BHP would "continue to look at all options for growth, both organic and inorganic," and that the current copper price environment — with LME copper crossing $11,000 per tonne for the first time since 2024 — had improved the economics of potential acquisitions.

"If we can find a counter-party where value creation for both sides is achievable, and where the asset fits our portfolio and our operating capabilities, we will pursue it," he said. "The demand outlook for copper over the next decade is as compelling as anything we have seen."

BHP's comments fuelled renewed speculation about potential targets. Analysts at Macquarie identified five mid-tier copper developers that could be logical acquisition candidates: Lundin Mining, Hudbay Minerals, Ero Copper, Arizona Copper, and Copper Mountain Mining. All five stocks rose between 3% and 8% on Monday as investors bid up potential takeout premiums.

BHP's existing copper growth pipeline centres on the Olympic Dam expansion in South Australia and the Resolution copper project in Arizona, a joint venture with Rio Tinto. Both projects face long development timelines and significant capital requirements, prompting analysts to question whether they are sufficient to position BHP for the copper supercycle that many in the industry now believe is underway.

Last year's failed $49 billion takeover attempt of Anglo American — which would have added the Quellaveco and Los Bronces copper mines to BHP's portfolio — illustrated the strategic imperative the company places on copper growth. Anglo rebuffed BHP's three bids.

Rio Tinto has also been active in the copper M&A market, acquiring the Nuton copper technologies business and taking a stake in the Winu copper-gold project in Western Australia. Glencore has indicated it remains focused on developing its existing copper assets in the DRC and South America rather than pursuing large acquisitions.

The intensifying M&A interest in copper reflects a broader industry consensus that the metal will be among the most critical commodities of the 2020s and 2030s, underpinned by AI infrastructure, electric vehicles and grid decarbonisation.

Tags: BHP, copper, M&A, acquisition, Mike Henry, mining, Lundin, Hudbay, Ero Copper, supercycle, AI, data center

Sentiment signal: bullish
